scope: B5.1 SSL-encoder canary; development-only; NOT confirmatory; NO tag

positive_control_first.interpretation: no decision-level separation -> inconclusive for safety (learned-encoder negative)

null_cov_type_I.caveat: cohorts share subjects; needs subject-cluster bound for a safe claim; only meaningful if POS separates

red_team_caveat.verdict: posfirst PASS; overclaim MINOR_ISSUE -> commit diagnostic-only; SCOPE the framing
red_team_caveat.supported: The fitted-null under-dispersion FAIL is fully INTACT under this learned SSL encoder: NULL_cov false-confirm 0.225 (~9x the 0.025 budget), fixed-margin-p floor-pinned in 92.5% of no-concept cohorts, T_z 6-9, and the no-concept(NULL_cov 6.35) > concept(POS 3.67) T_z INVERSION preserved (covariate-driven firing, same as B5.0).
red_team_caveat.NOT_a_strong_negative: The positive-control separation test is UNDERPOWERED: POS_concept 24/80 (0.30) vs NULL_cov 18/80 (0.225), Fisher one-sided p=0.1846, Newcombe diff-CI [-0.19,+0.34] spans 0, ~24% power at n=80 (needs true POS ~0.42+ for 80%). n>=60 was set to CONFIRM a FAIL, not to power separation. So this is INCONCLUSIVE for separation, NOT affirmative proof that learned features fail.
red_team_caveat.do_not_read_as: POS 0.30 > B5.0's 0.20 / > NULL 0.225 is NOT 'learned features help' (p=0.23 vs B5.0, p=0.18 vs NULL, both n.s.); POS_concept_plus_cov 0.25 also fails to separate from NULL (p=0.43). Do NOT comparator-shop the POS vs NULL_cov_plus_label p=0.018 cell.
red_team_caveat.scope: 'feature richness is not the lever' is supported for RANDOM (B5.0) + THIS ONE SSL EEGNet encoder only (n=1 learned family), NOT universal.
red_team_caveat.type_I_caveat: NULL_cov CP95u 0.315 / cohort-boot CI anti-conservative (30 of 54 subjects shared); needs subject-cluster bound; moot since POS does not separate.
red_team_caveat.defensible_conclusion: Even a learned label-free SSL EEGNet representation leaves the certifier's fitted-null under-dispersion FAIL fully intact and does NOT clear the positive-control gate (underpowered, p=0.18, ~24% power); feature richness -- random or this learned encoder -- is not the lever, while the study is too underpowered to be leaned on as affirmative proof that learned features fail.
